_metadata.description: HVAC quick reference data for field technicians
_metadata.disclaimer: Always verify with manufacturer specifications. Values are typical ranges.

superheat_targets.description: Target superheat ranges by metering device type

fixed_orifice_piston.method: Use superheat charging chart (outdoor ambient temp vs indoor wet bulb)
notes: Superheat varies by outdoor ambient and indoor wet bulb - always use charging chart
Lower ambient = higher target superheat
Higher ambient = lower target superheat
Below 65F outdoor ambient, superheat method becomes unreliable - use weigh-in method
Fixed orifice systems: superheat is the PRIMARY charging indicator

charging_chart_reference.description: Approximate target superheat by outdoor temp and indoor wet bulb
charging_chart_reference.note: These are approximations - use manufacturer chart for exact values

notes: TXV controls superheat - should remain relatively constant regardless of load
Superheat significantly outside 8-12F range indicates TXV issue, not charge issue
Use subcooling as the PRIMARY charging indicator on TXV systems
High superheat with TXV: check TXV bulb placement, insulation, or stuck valve
Low superheat with TXV: check for overcharge, TXV stuck open, or flooded evaporator
EEV adjusts dynamically - tighter superheat control than TXV
Variable speed systems typically target lower superheat
Superheat controlled by system controller, not just valve
Refer to manufacturer specs for specific EEV superheat targets

subcooling_targets.description: Target subcooling ranges by system type

notes: Subcooling is the PRIMARY charging method for TXV systems
Low subcooling = undercharged (or restriction before condenser outlet)
High subcooling = overcharged (or airflow issue over condenser)
Always verify condenser is clean and outdoor fan is running before adjusting charge
Subcooling changes approximately 2-3F per pound of refrigerant in residential systems
Subcooling is NOT the primary charging method for fixed orifice - use superheat
Subcooling is more variable on fixed orifice systems
Can be used as a secondary reference point
In heating mode, the indoor coil is the condenser
Subcooling measured at indoor coil liquid line
Lower subcooling targets in heating mode are normal

motor_winding_resistance.description: Typical motor winding resistance ranges by horsepower (single phase PSC motors, 230V)
notes: Measure C-R (common to run), C-S (common to start), and R-S (run to start)
R-S should approximately equal C-R + C-S
Infinite reading (OL) = open winding
Zero or near-zero reading = shorted winding
Any reading to ground = grounded winding (failed motor)
Values vary by manufacturer - these are typical ranges for reference

compressor_windings_single_phase.note: Compressor winding resistance is very low - use a quality ohmmeter
compressor_windings_single_phase.note2: Always compare measured values to manufacturer data on compressor nameplate or spec sheet

three_phase_motors.note: All three windings (T1-T2, T2-T3, T1-T3) should read within 5% of each other

capacitor_testing.pass_fail_rule: If measured uF is within +/-6% of rated value, capacitor passes. Outside +/-6% = replace.

examples.note: 5 uF +/-6% = 4.7 to 5.3 uF (common fan motor cap)
70 uF +/-6% = 65.8 to 74.2 uF (common on larger compressors)

dual_run_capacitors.description: Dual run capacitors have two sections - test each independently
dual_run_capacitors.testing: Test HERM-to-C for compressor uF, FAN-to-C for fan uF. Both must be within +/-6%.

voltage_rating_notes: Always replace with same or HIGHER voltage rating - never lower
370V cap can be replaced with 440V cap (not the reverse)
Capacitor uF must match rated value - do not upsize or downsize
safety_warnings: ALWAYS discharge capacitor before testing (short HERM to C and FAN to C with insulated jumper)
Capacitors can hold lethal charge even with power off
Use a 20,000 ohm 5-watt resistor for safe discharge
Never short terminals with a screwdriver - can damage capacitor or cause arc flash

compression_ratios.description: Typical compression ratios by refrigerant and application
compression_ratios.formula: Compression Ratio = Absolute Discharge Pressure / Absolute Suction Pressure (both in psia)
compression_ratios.psia_note: psia = psig + 14.7 (add atmospheric pressure to gauge reading)

R-22_residential_AC.example: Suction 70 psig (84.7 psia), Discharge 250 psig (264.7 psia) = 3.12:1

R-410A_residential_AC.example: Suction 120 psig (134.7 psia), Discharge 400 psig (414.7 psia) = 3.08:1

R-454B_residential_AC.example: Suction 115 psig (129.7 psia), Discharge 380 psig (394.7 psia) = 3.04:1

R-22_low_temp_refrigeration.note: High ratios reduce volumetric efficiency - consider two-stage

R-410A_heat_pump_heating.note: Higher ratio than cooling due to lower suction pressure in cold weather

guidelines: Compression ratio above 4:1 in AC applications indicates a problem
High ratio causes high discharge temp, low efficiency, compressor wear
Low suction pressure or high head pressure increases ratio
Dirty condenser, low airflow, or overcharge raises head pressure (increases ratio)
Low charge, restricted metering device, or low airflow lowers suction (increases ratio)
Maximum compression ratio for most reciprocating compressors: 10:1
Maximum compression ratio for most scroll compressors: 12:1

amp_draw_by_tonnage.description: Typical full load amp (FLA) ranges by system tonnage and voltage
amp_draw_by_tonnage.disclaimer: Always check nameplate - these are approximate ranges for reference only

blower_motor_indoor_unit.note: ECM motors draw less amps than PSC motors at the same HP

locked_rotor_amps.description: LRA is 4-6x the FLA - if amps stay at LRA, compressor is locked
locked_rotor_amps.note: If amp draw stays above FLA for more than a few seconds, check start components (capacitor, relay, hard start kit)

high_amps_causes: Low voltage supply (amps increase to compensate)
amp_draw_troubleshooting.rule_of_thumb: Amps should be within 10% of nameplate FLA under normal operating conditions

temperature_splits.description: Expected temperature difference across indoor coil (return air minus supply air)

notes: Measure at register closest to unit and at return
Low split: check charge, airflow, dirty coil, blower speed
High split: check airflow restriction, dirty filter, undersized ductwork
Split varies with humidity - higher latent load = lower sensible split
Temperature rise MUST be within range stamped on furnace rating plate
Low rise: check gas pressure, burner operation, blower speed too high
High rise: check filter, ductwork restriction, blower speed too low
Lower output temp than gas furnace - this is normal
Split decreases as outdoor temp drops
Auxiliary/emergency heat increases split when active

ohms_law.voltage: V = I x R (Volts = Amps x Ohms)
ohms_law.power: W = V x I (Watts = Volts x Amps)
ohms_law.power_3phase: W = V x I x 1.732 x PF (three-phase)

voltage_tolerances.note: Measure voltage at unit disconnect under load for accuracy
